summary: Crisp‑ish soundboard capture of an Alpine Valley night where Hey Pocky Way, Believe It Or Not and a thunderous Morning Dew shine despite odd vocal flanging.
review: The performance is widely hailed as one of the highlights of the 1988 Dust Bowl run – debuting “Believe It Or Not,” opening the second set with an incendiary Hey Pocky Way, and delivering a monumental Morning Dew.  The soundboard source is generally clear and lively, though many note a pervasive chorus/flanger effect on vocals that can feel underwater at times.

ai_rating.rationale: Over 50 reviews, the vast majority are 4‑5 ★ and praise the musical performance; technical complaints about flanging are consistent but do not outweigh the strong show appraisal.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Generally crisp with plenty of bass and clear drums, but a noticeable stereo chorus/flanger on vocals (often described as “underwater” or “phasing”) detracts for some listeners.

show_quality.setlist_flow: The set opens with a fresh “Hey Pocky Way,” rolls through a strong first‑set block (Roses → Masterpiece → Bird Song → Promised Land), and the second set builds to an emotional peak with the debut of “Believe It Or Not” and a thunderous “Morning Dew”, ending on a gentle Blackbird/Brokedown encore.

band_member_comments.Jerry: His vocal delivery on “Believe It Or Not” and the wailing on “Morning Dew” are repeatedly called emotional; early reviewers also praised his guitar tone.
band_member_comments.Phil: Bass described as “thunder bass”; noted big chord work during the Dew and occasional rhythm tightness issues.
band_member_comments.Bob: Weir’s vocals sometimes sounded off due to the flanging, but his lead on “Watchtower” and overall energy were praised.
band_member_comments.Keys: Brent (keys) gets multiple shout‑outs for backing vocals, laughter on Blackbird, and energizing moments in the second set.
band_member_comments.Drums: Kick drum is highlighted as strong and clear; later reviews note interesting drum textures during the Dew.
